text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own workshop (you had to be married to do this). Over the next five years, his art started mixing in Italian styles with the Northern styles he knew. Durer's daddy passed away in 1502, and his mama in 1513. The best stuff he made early on in his workshop was his woodcut prints, mostly about religious stuff, but he also did things like The Men's Bath House around 1496. These cuts were bigger and more detailed than most German woodcuts before, and they were more complex and balanced. Folks now think Durer probably didn’t cut the woodblocks himself; a professional would've handled that. But, learning in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and designed and cut woodblocks, really taught him a lot about what you could do with the technique and how to work with the folks cutting the blocks. Durer either drew right on the woodblock or stuck a paper drawing on it. Either way, his drawings got ruined when they cut the block.

text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own shop (he had to be married to do this). The best pieces he made early on in the shop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religious stuff, but also some everyday scenes like The Men's Bath House (around 1496). These cuts were bigger and more detailed than most German woodcuts before them, and they were way more complex and balanced in how they were set up. Folks now think Durer probably didn't cut any of the woodblocks himself; a specialist would've done that part. But, his training in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and handled both designing and cutting woodblocks for prints, clearly taught him a lot about what you could do with the technique, and how to work with the folks who cut the blocks. Durer either sketched his design right on the woodblock, or he stuck a paper drawing on it. Either way, his drawings got ruined when they cut the block.

text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own spot (had to be married to do that). Over the next five years, his style started mixing Italian vibes with Northern styles. Durer’s pops passed away in 1502, and his moms in 1513. The top pieces from the early days of his workshop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religion, but he had some other stuff too like The Men’s Bath House (around 1496). These were bigger and sharper than most German woodcuts before, and they were way more complex and balanced. People think now that Durer probably didn’t cut any of the woodblocks himself; a skilled craftsman would’ve done that. But, his training in Wolgemut’s studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and handled woodcuts, for sure taught him a lot about the craft and how to work with the woodblock cutters. Durer either drew his design straight on the woodblock, or he stuck a paper drawing on it. Either way, his drawings got wrecked when they cut the block.

text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own shop (had to be married to do that). Over the next five years, his style started mixing in Italian vibes with the Northern styles he knew. Durer's daddy passed in 1502, and his mama in 1513. The best stuff he made early on at the shop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religious stuff, but he threw in some everyday scenes too like The Men's Bath House (around 1496). These cuts were bigger and sharper than most German woodcuts before them, and they had a lot more going on and were better put together. Folks think now that Durer probably didn't cut none of those woodblocks himself; a skilled craftsman would’ve handled that. But, his training in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a bunch of carved and painted altarpieces and handled woodcuts too, really taught him what you could do with the technique and how to work with the block cutters. text_b: What impact did Alexander II's death have on the Russian political scene? It set off the end of the anarchist movement.

text_a: A stranger roll up in town, meet fine lil' Susan Martinez De La Cruz, and roll with her to a barbecue. Wealthy Jason Carberry up there talking 'bout recently dead Robin Randall, a citizen who got shot. Jason ain't feeling the stranger being there, since he look after Susan and be protective of her. He call him out to a shootout, but the stranger whip out his pistol 'fore Jason can even get his out the holster. Calaveras Kate, a saloon singer who dig Jason, relieved when the stranger ain't shoot. Rafael Moreno come through all sudden, starting beef with the stranger. They go at it till Judge Wallace Wintrop and his niece Sheila, who just got here from back East, show up disgusted by all the wild violence in the West. Turns out the stranger is Reb Randall, the dead dude's brother. He on the hunt for the killer, who might be Rafael, Jason, or even Billy Buckett, the coward of the county. The women holding they breath, hoping they men make it through.
text_b: Who was Robin Randall's brother? Da stranger

text_a: In India to cop some horses, British aristocrat, Lord Esketh and his wife, Edwina, hit up the town of Ranchipur 'cause the old Maharani invited them. They ain't happy together and Lord Esketh 'bout to dip back to England and start them divorce papers. Edwina, all spoiled and cold, just laughs it off. She link up again with her old flame, Tom Ransome, in Ranchipur, who now just be drinking his life away. She also tryna throw game at a respected Hindu doctor, Dr. Rama Safti, a solid dude who the old Maharani picked to take over for her one day. Safti try to hold back, but eventually, he can't resist Edwina's game and falls hard for her. Lord Esketh finds out, but Safti saves him from a wild tiger during a safari. Safti confess his feelings for Edwina to Lord Esketh, who starts feeling bad for the good doctor. Ransome feel the same, telling Edwina to back off from Safti, 'cause he got mad respect for him. Edwina ends up losing favor with the Maharani too, who tells her that Safti
text_b: Who tryna seduce the dude who saved Lord Esketh from a man-eatin' tiger on a safari? Lord Esketh's wife

text_a: The rally happened on October 17, the shooting on February 29. text_a: (CNN) -- Callin' 2010 "The best year in safety performance in our company's history," Transocean Ltd., owner of the Gulf of Mexico oil rig that exploded, killin' 11 workers, done gave its top executives big bonuses and raises, 'cording to a recent filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. That includes a $200,000 salary increase for Transocean president and chief executive officer Steven L. Newman, whose base salary gon' increase from $900,000 to $1.1 million, 'cording to the SEC report. Newman's bonus was $374,062, the report states. Newman also got a $5.4 million long-term compensation package the company gave him when he got appointed as CEO in March 2010, 'cording to the SEC filing. The latest cash awards are based in part on the company's "performance under safety," the Transocean filing states. "Despite the tragic loss of life in the Gulf of Mexico, we achieved an exemplary statistical safety record as measured by our total recordable incident rate and total potential severity rate," the SEC statement reads. "As measured by these standards, we recorded the best year in safety performance in our Company's history
text_b: What's the name of the oil rig in the Gulf of Mexico? Transocean Ltd

text_a: Taliban and American aid. Never mind the fact that authors like Robert Scheer been warnin' 'bout helpin' the Taliban way back in May 2001. Never mind they ain't do it outta no kind heart, but because the Taliban was harsh 'bout stoppin' them opium poppies. Never mind in a regime run by warlords, it don't matter who supposed to hand out the aid—the big boss warlords gon' snatch it and use it for they own good. Never mind this same talk been used by them hawks against sendin' aid to Iraq's Saddam Hussein. Never mind the Taliban kept on sellin' opium despite the deal. Never mind all this is laid out on Michael Moore's website 'bout the film. Gun homicides. Statistics are Moore's weakest point, and it's wild that his critics don't harp on 'em more. Jerry wake up just in time to dodge the 10-ball and get carried out to the ball return, where the 10 and the 13 ball sandwich Jerry in between. Jerry heated and walks up through the pocket, don't see nothin' at first, but then he step back to the pocket and catch Tom hidin' behind it. Tom blast all them balls one after another with his cue, then aim for Jerry, but the mouse cling to the cue tip. The cat, like he sayin' "Have it your way", chalks up and use Jerry to shoot the 8

text_a: While errythang was movin' along, folks was still tryna handle they diplomacy biz. Puttin' direct pressure on the Taliban ain't work out. Like one NSC staff note said, "Under the Taliban, Afghanistan ain't really a state sponsor of terrorism, it's more like a state sponsored by terrorists." Early in 2000, the United States started really pushin' to get Pakistan to influence the Taliban. In January 2000, Assistant Secretary of State Karl Inderfurth and the State Department's counterterrorism coordinator, Michael Sheehan, chopped it up with General Musharraf in Islamabad, tempting him with the chance of a presidential visit in March as a thank you for Pakistan's help. Musharraf was really feenin' for that visit, partly cuz it would make his government look legit. He told the diplomats he'd holla at Mullah Omar and press him on Bin Laden. But when they dipped, they reported back to Washington that Pakistan probably ain't gon' do much, given how they see the benefits of the Taliban runnin' Afghanistan. President Clinton was 'posed to head to India. The State Department was like, he shouldn't hit up India without also stopping by Pakistan. But the Secret Service
text_b: What was two reasons the Secret Service, the CIA, and counterterrorism folks told President Clinton not to hit up Pakistan? The visit gon' put the President's life in danger and Pakistan ain't done enough to earn the visit?

text_a: Malaquez's spot done opened up to show a greenmunk trapped in some solid air. Leaf bits was scatterin' as he dashed to meet a buddy or someone with some grub. Tasha was all amazed. I was like, "Frodo been comin' 'round here, huh?" Malaquez was like, "Your pet?" "Nah, he just be around. Probably came 'cause of all that noise up the hill." "Ah," Malaquez said. "Why 'Frodo'?" Tasha was like, "A lil' dude with big, furry feet. What else you gon' call him?" She passed that sculpture to me. I almost dropped that thing; thought it'd be light like a holo. "Heavy," I said, like he ain't know. He cracked up. "My last piece was four old Undersiders huddled 'round a trash fire. Be glad that ain't what you caught." He was all excited talkin' 'bout his art like a kid. "Um, I should wait to bug you, but..."
text_b: Where Frodo stay at? Somewhere 'round the hill
